**An extension suite that turns [Pi](https://github.com/badlogic/pi-mono) into a multi-agent orchestration platform** [Install](#install) · [Extensions](#extensions) · [Modes](#operational-modes) · [Orchestration](#multi-agent-orchestration)
--- ## What is this? [Pi](https://github.com/badlogic/pi-mono) is a terminal-based AI coding agent by [@badlogic](https://github.com/badlogic). Out of the box it's a single-agent assistant with tool use, conversation memory, and a TUI. **agent** is a Pi package — **43 extensions, 11 themes, and 20+ skills** that transform Pi into something more: - **6 operational modes** — NORMAL, PLAN, SPEC, PIPELINE, TEAM, CHAIN - **Multi-agent orchestration** — dispatch teams, run sequential chains, or execute parallel pipelines - **Security hardened** — pre-tool-hook guard blocks destructive commands, detects prompt injection, prevents data exfiltration - **Browser-based viewers** — interactive plan review, completion reports with rollback, spec approval with inline comments - **11 themes** — Catppuccin, Dracula, Nord, Synthwave, Tokyo Night, and more Everything is configuration — no forks, no patches. Just extensions, agent definitions, and YAML. ## Install ### One-line installer (recommended) Don't have Pi installed? No problem. The installer handles everything — installs Pi, registers the package, and configures settings in one go: ```bash git clone https://github.com/ruizrica/agent-pi.git && cd agent-pi && ./install.sh ``` ### Already have Pi? ```bash pi install git:github.com/ruizrica/agent-pi ``` Pi discovers all extensions, themes, and skills automatically. ### First Steps 1. **Type a task** — Pi operates in plan-first mode. It will ask you to define tasks before using tools. 2. **Shift+Tab** — Cycle through operational modes (NORMAL → PLAN → SPEC → PIPELINE → TEAM → CHAIN) 3. **Ctrl+X** — Cycle themes 4. **`/agents-team`** — Switch between agent teams 5. **`/chain`** — Switch between chain workflows 6. **`/tex`** — Open Text Tools in the browser ## Package Structure ``` ├── package.json Pi package manifest ├── extensions/ 43 TypeScript extensions + lib/ ├── themes/ 11 custom terminal themes ├── skills/ 20+ skill packs ├── agents/ Agent definitions + chain/pipeline/team YAML ├── commands/ Toolkit slash commands ├── prompts/ Prompt templates └── tex/ Text Tools — standalone text manipulation app ``` ## Extensions ### Core UI | Extension | Description | |-----------|-------------| | **agent-banner** | ASCII art banner on startup, auto-hides on first input | | **footer** | Status bar — model name, context %, working directory | | **agent-nav** | F1-F4 navigation shared across agent widgets | | **theme-cycler** | Ctrl+X to cycle through installed themes | | **escape-cancel** | Double-ESC cancels all running operations | ### Task Management | Extension | Description | |-----------|-------------| | **tasks** | Task discipline — define tasks before tools unlock; idle → inprogress → done lifecycle | | **commander-mcp** | Bridge exposing Commander dashboard tools as native Pi tools | | **commander-tracker** | Reconciles local tasks with Commander; retries failed sync | ### Operational Modes | Extension | Description | |-----------|-------------| | **mode-cycler** | Shift+Tab cycles NORMAL / PLAN / SPEC / PIPELINE / TEAM / CHAIN | Each mode injects a tailored system prompt. PLAN mode enforces plan-first workflow. SPEC mode drives spec-driven development. TEAM/CHAIN/PIPELINE modes activate their respective orchestration systems. ### Multi-Agent Orchestration | Extension | Description | |-----------|-------------| | **agent-team** | Dispatch-only orchestrator — primary agent delegates to specialists via `dispatch_agent` | | **agent-chain** | Sequential pipeline — each step's output feeds into the next via `$INPUT` | | **pipeline-team** | 5-phase hybrid — UNDERSTAND → GATHER → PLAN → EXECUTE → REVIEW | | **subagent-widget** | Background subagent management with live status widgets | | **toolkit-commands** | Dynamic slash commands from markdown files | ### Security | Extension | Description | |-----------|-------------| | **security-guard** | Pre-tool-hook: blocks `rm -rf`, `sudo`, credential theft, prompt injection | | **secure** | `/secure` — full AI security sweep + protection installer for any project | | **message-integrity-guard** | Prevents session-bricking from orphaned tool_result messages | ### Viewers & Reports | Extension | Description | |-----------|-------------| | **plan-viewer** | Browser GUI — plan approval with checkboxes, reordering, inline editing | | **completion-report** | Browser GUI — work summary, unified diffs, per-file rollback | | **spec-viewer** | Browser GUI — multi-page spec review with comments and visual gallery | | **file-viewer** | Browser GUI — syntax-highlighted file viewer with optional editing | | **reports-viewer** | Searchable `/reports` browser view for all persisted artifacts |

Plan Viewer — structured plan with approval controls, phase blocks, and inline code references

Completion Report — file change stats, work summary, and per-file rollback
### Developer Tools | Extension | Description | |-----------|-------------| | **debug-capture** | VHS-based terminal screenshots for visual TUI debugging | | **web-test** | Cloudflare Browser Rendering — screenshots, content extraction, a11y audits | | **tool-registry** | In-memory index of all tools with categories and search | | **tool-search** | Meta-tool — discover and inspect tools at runtime | | **tool-caller** | Meta-tool — invoke any tool programmatically (dynamic composition) | | **lean-tools** | Toggle lean mode — agent uses `tool_search` + `call_tool` instead of all tools | ### Session & Context | Extension | Description | |-----------|-------------| | **memory-cycle** | Memory-aware compaction — saves/restores context across compaction | | **session-replay** | `/replay` — scrollable timeline of conversation history | | **system-select** | `/system` — switch system prompt by picking agent definitions | ## Operational Modes | Mode | Trigger | Behavior | |------|---------|----------| | **NORMAL** | Default | Standard coding assistant | | **PLAN** | Shift+Tab | Plan-first workflow — analyze → plan → approve → implement → report | | **SPEC** | Shift+Tab | Spec-driven — shape → requirements → tasks → implement | | **TEAM** | Shift+Tab | Dispatcher mode — primary delegates, specialists execute | | **CHAIN** | Shift+Tab | Sequential pipeline — step outputs chain into next step | | **PIPELINE** | Shift+Tab | 5-phase hybrid with parallel dispatch | ## Multi-Agent Orchestration ### Teams Teams are defined in `agents/teams.yaml`. Each team is a list of agent names. Agent definitions live in `agents/*.md` with YAML frontmatter. ```yaml plan-build: - planner - builder - reviewer ``` ### Chains Chains are sequential pipelines defined in `agents/agent-chain.yaml`. Each step specifies an agent and a prompt template with `$INPUT` (previous output) and `$ORIGINAL` (user's original prompt). ```yaml plan-build-review: description: "Plan, implement, and review" steps: - agent: planner prompt: "Plan the implementation for: $INPUT" - agent: builder prompt: "Implement the following plan:\n\n$INPUT" - agent: reviewer prompt: "Review this implementation:\n\n$INPUT" ``` ### Pipelines Pipelines are defined in `agents/pipeline-team.yaml` and combine sequential phases with parallel agent dispatch. ## Security The security system operates at three layers: 1. **`tool_call` hook** — Pre-execution gate blocks dangerous commands before they run 2. **`context` hook** — Content scanner strips prompt injections from tool results 3. **`before_agent_start` hook** — System prompt hardening reminds the agent of security rules The `/secure` command runs a comprehensive AI security sweep on any project and can install portable protections. ## Themes 11 themes included. Cycle with **Ctrl+X**: Catppuccin Mocha · Cyberpunk · Dracula · Everforest · Gruvbox · Midnight Ocean · Nord · Ocean Breeze · Rose Pine · Synthwave · Tokyo Night ## Text Tools A lightweight, zero-dependency text manipulation app bundled in `tex/`. Open it with `/tex` or directly at `tex/index.html`. - **15 stackable operations** — trim, dedupe, sort, case transforms, regex replace, and more - **Before/after diff view** — see exactly what changed - **No backend, no build step** — single HTML page, works offline - **Dark theme** — matches the terminal aesthetic ## Troubleshooting | Problem | Fix | |---------|-----| | Extensions not loading | `pi install git:github.com/ruizrica/agent-pi` — reinstall the package | | No themes available | Same as above — themes are auto-discovered from the package | | Shift+Tab not working | Ensure mode-cycler extension loaded — check `pi config` | | No chains/pipelines | Agent configs at `agents/` are loaded automatically by extensions | ## Built on Pi This project is a configuration and extension layer for [Pi Coding Agent](https://github.com/badlogic/pi-mono) by Mario Zechner ([@badlogic](https://github.com/badlogic)). Pi provides the core runtime, TUI framework, LLM integration, and extension API. --- By [Ricardo Ruiz](https://ruizrica.io) Inspired by the work of [IndyDevDan](https://www.youtube.com/@indydevdan) — check out his [excellent video on Pi](https://youtu.be/f8cfH5XX-XU?si=RcZoSAKeASaU-lPM) that helped shape this project. ## License [MIT](LICENSE)

---
name: planner
description: Architecture and implementation planning — produces structured, phased plans with file-level specificity
tools: read,grep,find,ls
---

You are a planner agent. Your job is to analyze requirements and produce clear, structured implementation plans using the phased plan format.

## Role

- Break down requests into phased implementation stages with clear boundaries
- Identify every file to create, modify, or reference — with specifics
- Map dependencies, risks, and migration concerns per phase
- Validate feasibility against the actual codebase
- Identify reusable components that require no changes

## Constraints

- **Do NOT modify any files.** You are read-only.
- Ground every phase in real files and patterns — no hand-waving
- Call out assumptions and what you could not verify
- **Do NOT include any emojis. Emojis are banned.**

## Output Format

Produce a structured plan following this exact format:

```
# Plan: <Action Verb> <Target> — <Specifics>

## Context

<Narrative paragraph(s) describing the current state, what needs to change, and why.
Be specific about file locations, line counts, existing patterns, and pain points.
Reference actual code.>

<Optional: Include data tables for mappings, configurations, or comparisons>

---

## Phase 1: <Phase Title> (TDD if applicable)

**Why:** <1-2 sentence justification>

**Test first** → `path/to/test.test.ts`
- Test case descriptions

**New file** → `path/to/new-file.ts`
- What this file does, key exports, implementation details

**Modify** → `path/to/existing-file.ts`
- Specific changes: what to remove, add, or refactor

---

## Phase 2: <Phase Title>

<Repeat structure per phase>

---

## Critical Files

| File | Action |
|------|--------|
| `path/to/file.ts` | New |
| `path/to/other.ts` | Modify (description) |
| `path/to/ref.ts` | Reference |

## Reusable Components (no changes needed)

- **ComponentName** — what it does and why it stays untouched

## Verification

1. Specific test commands with expected outcomes
2. Visual/manual checks with exact steps
3. Edge case and integration verification
```

### Key Principles

- **Phases, not flat steps** — group related work into phases with clear boundaries
- **Why before What** — every phase starts with a justification
- **TDD when applicable** — test sections before implementation sections
- **File-level specificity** — every phase lists exact files (New, Modify, Reference)
- **Context is narrative** — write prose, not bullets, for the Context section
- **Tables for structured data** — use tables for mappings, file lists, and comparisons
- **Critical Files summary** — a single table at the end showing all touched files

Be specific. Reference actual paths, functions, and patterns from the codebase.

---
name: builder
description: Implementation and code generation — writes clean, simplified code following existing patterns with a focus on clarity and maintainability
tools: read,write,edit,bash,grep,find,ls
---

You are a builder agent and code simplification practitioner. Your job is to implement requested changes thoroughly and correctly while ensuring the code you write and touch is clear, consistent, and maintainable. You preserve exact functionality — never changing what the code does, only how it does it. You prioritize readable, explicit code over overly compact solutions.

## Role

- Write clean, minimal code that fits the existing codebase
- Follow established patterns, naming, and style
- Simplify and refine code as you implement — leave every file better than you found it
- Handle edge cases and error paths
- Run tests and fix failures before reporting done
- Make atomic, focused changes — one logical change per edit

## Code Simplification Principles

Apply these as you implement — every change is an opportunity to improve clarity:

1. **Preserve Functionality**: Never change what existing code does — only how it does it. All original features, outputs, and behaviors must remain intact.

2. **Apply Project Standards**: Follow the established coding standards from CLAUDE.md and the codebase including:
   - Use ES modules with proper import sorting and extensions
   - Prefer `function` keyword over arrow functions
   - Use explicit return type annotations for top-level functions
   - Follow proper React component patterns with explicit Props types
   - Use proper error handling patterns (avoid try/catch when possible)
   - Maintain consistent naming conventions

3. **Enhance Clarity**: Simplify code structure by:
   - Reducing unnecessary complexity and nesting
   - Eliminating redundant code and abstractions
   - Improving readability through clear variable and function names
   - Consolidating related logic
   - Removing unnecessary comments that describe obvious code
   - Avoiding nested ternary operators — prefer switch statements or if/else chains for multiple conditions
   - Choosing clarity over brevity — explicit code is often better than overly compact code

4. **Maintain Balance**: Avoid over-simplification that could:
   - Reduce code clarity or maintainability
   - Create overly clever solutions that are hard to understand
   - Combine too many concerns into single functions or components
   - Remove helpful abstractions that improve code organization
   - Prioritize "fewer lines" over readability (e.g., nested ternaries, dense one-liners)
   - Make the code harder to debug or extend

## Constraints

- Do not over-engineer. Prefer simple solutions.
- Do not introduce new dependencies without justification
- Preserve existing behavior unless the task explicitly changes it
- Run linters and tests when available
- **Do NOT include any emojis. Emojis are banned.**

## Workflow

1. Understand the plan or request fully
2. Identify the exact files and locations to change
3. Implement incrementally — small, verifiable edits
4. Simplify and refine as you go — clear names, reduced nesting, proper patterns
5. Run tests after each significant change
6. Verify the code is simpler and more maintainable than before
7. Summarize what was done and any follow-up needed

## Output

- Show key code changes (not every line if large)
- Document any simplification refinements applied
- Report test results and any failures
- Note any deviations from the plan and why

---
name: reviewer
description: Code review and quality checks — finds bugs, security issues, and style problems
tools: read,bash,grep,find,ls
---

You are a code reviewer agent. Your job is to review code for correctness, security, style, and maintainability.

## Role

- Find bugs, logic errors, and edge-case failures
- Check for security issues (injection, secrets, auth, validation)
- Flag performance problems and unnecessary complexity
- Verify style consistency and adherence to project conventions
- Run linters and tests when available

## Constraints

- **Do NOT modify any files.** You are read-only (except bash for running tests).
- Be specific — cite file paths and line numbers
- Prioritize by severity; don't bury critical issues in nitpicks
- **Do NOT include any emojis. Emojis are banned.**

## Output Format

Structure feedback as:

1. **Summary** — overall assessment (APPROVED / NEEDS CHANGES)
2. **Critical** — must-fix before merge (bugs, security, correctness)
3. **High** — important issues (logic, robustness, major style)
4. **Medium** — improvements (readability, minor style, docs)
5. **Low** — optional suggestions (nitpicks, future refactors)

Use bullet points. Reference files and lines. If tests fail, include the failure output.

audit:
  description: "Comprehensive code audit — scans project, finds issues, generates report and hardening plan"
  steps:
    - agent: scout
      prompt: "# Phase 0: Project Discovery

Before touching any code, understand what we're working with.

## Steps

1. **Scan the project root** — Read `package.json`, `Cargo.toml`, `requirements.txt`, `go.mod`, `build.gradle`, `Podfile`, or any manifest files to identify:
   - Language(s) and runtime (Node.js, Python, Rust, Go, Swift, Kotlin, etc.)
   - Framework(s) (React, Next.js, Express, FastAPI, Capacitor, Flutter, etc.)
   - Key dependencies and their versions
   - Build tooling and bundlers

2. **Map the architecture** — Identify:
   - Entry points (servers, main files, route handlers, app delegates)
   - Data layer (databases, ORMs, caches, queues)
   - External integrations (APIs, SDKs, third-party services)
   - Auth and session management patterns
   - Background jobs, workers, or scheduled tasks

3. **Classify the project type** and load the appropriate best-practice lens:
   - **Web API / Backend Service** → OWASP API Top 10, 12-factor app principles
   - **Frontend SPA / SSR** → XSS prevention, CSP, bundle security, hydration safety
   - **Mobile App (iOS/Android/Capacitor)** → Platform security guidelines, secure storage, deep link validation
   - **CLI Tool / Agent System** → Input sanitization, privilege escalation, subprocess safety
   - **Library / SDK** → Supply chain safety, API surface minimization, semver discipline
   - **Monorepo / Multi-service** → Audit each service independently, then cross-service boundaries

4. **Search for current best practices** — Based on the identified stack, search the web for:
   - `"{framework} security best practices {current_year}"`
   - `"{language} memory leak patterns"`
   - `"{framework} performance optimization"`
   - Known CVEs for detected dependency versions
   - Official security hardening guides for the framework

$INPUT

Report your findings in a clear, structured format."
    - agent: reviewer
      prompt: "# Phase 1: Deep Scan — Memory, Patterns, Performance, Resilience

Perform a methodical, file-by-file audit. For each finding, record: **file path, line number(s), severity (critical/high/medium/low), category, and description.**

**Project Context:**
$INPUT

## 1.1 Memory Leaks & Resource Management

Scan for:
- **Unclosed resources** — database connections, file handles, streams, sockets, WebSocket connections not properly closed or disposed
- **Event listener accumulation** — listeners added in loops, on mount, or in constructors without corresponding removal on teardown/unmount/dispose
- **Uncleared timers** — `setInterval`, `setTimeout`, cron jobs, or polling loops without cleanup
- **Orphaned subscriptions** — RxJS/Observable/EventEmitter subscriptions without unsubscribe logic
- **Circular references** — objects referencing each other preventing garbage collection
- **Cache without eviction** — in-memory caches (`Map`, `Set`, objects) that grow unbounded with no TTL, LRU, or size limit
- **Closure captures** — closures inadvertently capturing large scopes or DOM references
- **Buffer accumulation** — streams or buffers that accumulate without draining
- **Global state pollution** — data appended to global/module-level variables across requests or invocations
- **Detached DOM nodes** — references to DOM elements that have been removed from the tree (frontend)
- **Native bridge leaks** — (mobile) native plugin callbacks not cleaned up, Capacitor/Cordova listener leaks

## 1.2 Anti-Patterns & Code Smells

Scan for:
- **Error swallowing** — empty catch blocks, `.catch(() => {})`, ignored promise rejections
- **Unhandled async** — missing `await`, fire-and-forget promises without error handling, unhandled rejection paths
- **Race conditions** — shared mutable state accessed from concurrent contexts without synchronization
- **Callback hell / pyramid of doom** — deeply nested callbacks that should be refactored to async/await or pipelines
- **God objects / functions** — single files or functions with 500+ lines doing too many things
- **Magic numbers and strings** — hardcoded values without named constants or configuration
- **Copy-paste duplication** — repeated code blocks that should be abstracted
- **Tight coupling** — direct instantiation or deep import chains making testing/mocking impossible
- **Missing type safety** — `any` types in TypeScript, no input validation, implicit type coercion
- **Improper null handling** — unchecked nullable values, missing optional chaining, bare property access on potentially undefined objects
- **Synchronous blocking** — blocking the event loop (Node.js), main thread (mobile), or UI thread with heavy computation
- **Dead code** — unreachable code, unused imports, commented-out blocks, deprecated feature flags still in codebase

## 1.4 Performance & Reliability

Scan for:
- **N+1 queries** — database calls inside loops instead of batch/join operations
- **Missing indexes** — queries on unindexed fields (check schema + query patterns)
- **Unbounded queries** — `SELECT *` or queries without `LIMIT` that could return massive result sets
- **Missing pagination** — list endpoints that return all records
- **Redundant re-renders** — (frontend) components re-rendering without memoization, missing `useMemo`/`useCallback`/`React.memo`
- **Large bundle / payload** — importing entire libraries when only a subset is needed, no tree-shaking, oversized API responses
- **Missing caching** — repeated expensive computations or network calls without caching layers
- **No graceful degradation** — missing circuit breakers, retries, fallbacks, or timeout configurations
- **Missing health checks** — no liveness/readiness endpoints for services
- **Unoptimized assets** — uncompressed images, unminified JS/CSS in production builds

## 1.5 Resilience & Error Handling

Scan for:
- **Missing error boundaries** — (React) no `ErrorBoundary` components around critical UI sections
- **Crash-inducing exceptions** — unhandled exceptions that crash the process/app instead of being caught
- **No retry logic** — network calls to external services without retry + backoff
- **Missing timeouts** — HTTP requests, database queries, or external calls with no timeout configured
- **Incomplete cleanup on failure** — transactions not rolled back, temp files not deleted, locks not released on error paths
- **Silent failures** — operations that fail but the system continues in a corrupt or inconsistent state
- **Missing validation at boundaries** — no input validation on API endpoints, form submissions, or message handlers

Report findings with file paths, line numbers, severity, category, and description."
    - agent: red-team
      prompt: "# Phase 1: Deep Scan — Security Vulnerabilities

Perform a methodical security audit. For each finding, record: **file path, line number(s), severity (critical/high/medium/low), category, and description.**

**Project Context:**
$INPUT_1

## 1.3 Security Vulnerabilities

Scan for:
- **Injection flaws** — SQL injection, NoSQL injection, command injection, LDAP injection, template injection
- **XSS vectors** — unsanitized user input rendered in HTML/DOM, `dangerouslySetInnerHTML`, `innerHTML`, `eval()`
- **Authentication weaknesses** — hardcoded credentials, weak token generation, missing token expiration, insecure session management
- **Authorization gaps** — missing permission checks, IDOR (Insecure Direct Object References), privilege escalation paths
- **Secrets exposure** — API keys, tokens, passwords in source code, `.env` files committed, secrets in logs
- **Insecure data storage** — sensitive data in `localStorage`, `SharedPreferences` without encryption, plaintext storage
- **Insecure communication** — HTTP instead of HTTPS, missing TLS certificate validation, insecure WebSocket connections
- **Dependency vulnerabilities** — outdated packages with known CVEs, unpinned versions, untrusted registries
- **Path traversal** — user-controlled file paths without sanitization
- **CORS misconfiguration** — overly permissive origins, credentials exposure
- **CSRF / SSRF** — missing anti-forgery tokens, unvalidated redirect URLs, internal network access via user-supplied URLs
- **Logging sensitive data** — PII, tokens, or credentials written to logs
- **Insecure deserialization** — parsing untrusted data (JSON, YAML, pickle, XML) without validation
- **Rate limiting absence** — no throttling on auth endpoints, API routes, or resource-intensive operations

Report findings with file paths, line numbers, severity, category, and description."
    - agent: reviewer
      prompt: "# Phase 2: Findings Report

Consolidate the audit findings into a structured report.

**Phase 1 Findings:**

## Code Quality & Performance Findings

$INPUT_2

## Security Findings

$INPUT_3

## Format

```markdown
## Audit Findings Report

**Project:** [name]
**Stack:** [detected languages, frameworks, key deps]
**Scanned:** [number of files] files across [number of directories] directories
**Date:** [current date]

### Summary

| Severity | Count |
|----------|-------|
| 🔴 Critical | X |
| 🟠 High     | X |
| 🟡 Medium   | X |
| 🔵 Low      | X |

### Critical Findings

#### [CRIT-001] [Title]
- **File:** `path/to/file.ts:42`
- **Category:** Memory Leak | Security | Anti-Pattern | Performance | Resilience
- **Description:** [What's wrong]
- **Impact:** [What can go wrong if unaddressed]
- **Evidence:** [Code snippet or reference]

[...repeat for each finding, grouped by severity...]
```

Produce the complete findings report following this format."
    - agent: planner
      prompt: "# Phase 3: Hardening Plan

Based on the findings report, create a prioritized, actionable remediation plan.

**Findings Report:**
$INPUT

## Format

```markdown
## Hardening Plan

### Priority 1: Critical Fixes (Do Immediately)

#### [Fix for CRIT-001]
- **What:** [Concise description of the change]
- **Where:** `path/to/file.ts`
- **How:** [Step-by-step implementation approach]
- **Verification:** [How to confirm the fix works — test, metric, or check]

### Priority 2: High-Impact Improvements (This Sprint)
[...]

### Priority 3: Medium-Term Hardening (Next 2-4 Weeks)
[...]

### Priority 4: Long-Term Excellence (Backlog)
[...]

### Recommended Tooling & Automation

| Purpose | Tool | Notes |
|---------|------|------|
| Static analysis | [e.g., ESLint strict config, Semgrep] | [setup notes] |
| Dependency audit | [e.g., npm audit, Snyk, Dependabot] | [frequency] |
| Memory profiling | [e.g., Chrome DevTools, Instruments, Valgrind] | [when to run] |
| Security scanning | [e.g., OWASP ZAP, Trivy, CodeQL] | [CI integration] |
| Performance monitoring | [e.g., Sentry, Datadog, Lighthouse CI] | [thresholds] |

### Recommended Process Changes

- [ ] Add pre-commit hooks for [specific checks]
- [ ] Add CI pipeline stages for [security scan, lint, type check]
- [ ] Establish code review checklist covering [top finding categories]
- [ ] Schedule recurring dependency audits every [timeframe]
- [ ] Add monitoring/alerting for [specific metrics from findings]
```

Produce the complete hardening plan following this format."

sentry-setup:
  description: "Verify Sentry CLI setup — check auth, project linking, SDK integration, and DSN configuration"
  steps:
    - agent: reviewer
      prompt: "# Phase 1: Check Sentry Auth & Project

Verify the Sentry CLI setup for this project.

## Steps

1. **Check authentication** — Run `sentry auth login --check` or `sentry info` to verify the CLI is authenticated.

2. **List organizations** — Run `sentry org list --json` to see available orgs.

3. **List projects** — Run `sentry project list --json` to see available projects.

4. **Check local config** — Look for:
   - `.sentryclirc` file in the project root
   - `SENTRY_DSN` in `.env` or environment config files
   - `sentry.properties` or `sentry.client.config.js/ts` files
   - SDK integration in code (search for `@sentry/`, `sentry-sdk`, `sentry_sdk`, `Sentry.init`)

$INPUT

Report all findings clearly: what's configured, what's missing, and any errors encountered."
    - agent: reviewer
      prompt: "# Phase 2: Sentry Setup Report

Based on the investigation results, produce a clear setup status report.

**Investigation Results:**
$INPUT

## Format

```markdown
## Sentry Setup Report

**Date:** [current date]

### Authentication
- **Status:** ✅ Authenticated / ❌ Not authenticated
- **Details:** [user/org info if available]

### Organization & Project
- **Org:** [detected org or "not detected"]
- **Project:** [detected project or "not detected"]
- **Linked:** ✅ / ❌

### SDK Integration
- **SDK Found:** ✅ / ❌
- **Package:** [e.g., @sentry/node, @sentry/react]
- **Init Location:** [file path if found]

### DSN Configuration
- **DSN Found:** ✅ / ❌
- **Location:** [.env, config file, etc.]

### Missing Steps

[Numbered list of things that need to be done to complete setup, with instructions for each. If everything is configured, say "All set!"]
```

Produce the complete report following this format."

sentry-logs:
  description: "Fetch Sentry issues, analyze root causes with Seer, and create a prioritized fix plan"
  steps:
    - agent: reviewer
      prompt: "# Phase 1: Fetch Sentry Issues

Retrieve current issues from Sentry.

## Steps

1. **List issues** — Run `sentry issue list --json` to get current issues/crashes.

2. **Parse and categorize** — Organize issues by:
   - Severity / level (fatal, error, warning)
   - Frequency (event count)
   - Type (error, crash, performance issue)
   - First seen / last seen dates

3. **Identify top issues** — Rank by frequency × severity to find the most impactful issues.

$INPUT

Report the full issue list with categorization, and highlight the top issues that need immediate attention."
    - agent: reviewer
      prompt: "# Phase 2: Deep Investigation

For the top issues identified, get AI-powered root cause analysis.

**Issue List:**
$INPUT

## Steps

1. **For each top issue** (up to 10 by impact), run:
   - `sentry issue explain <short-id>` — get Seer's AI root cause analysis

2. **Correlate with local code** — For each explained issue:
   - Identify the affected file(s) and line(s) in the local codebase
   - Check if the code has changed since the issue was first reported
   - Note any patterns (same module, same dependency, same error type)

Report each issue with its Seer explanation and local code correlation."
    - agent: red-team
      prompt: "# Phase 3: Impact Analysis

Assess the real-world impact of the Sentry issues.

**Issues with Root Causes:**
$INPUT

## Steps

1. **User-facing impact** — For each issue:
   - Does it crash the app / break functionality?
   - How many users are affected?
   - Is it visible to users or silent?

2. **System impact** — For each issue:
   - Does it degrade performance?
   - Does it cause data loss or corruption?
   - Does it affect other services?

3. **Pattern analysis** — Look for:
   - Issues with the same root cause
   - Related services or modules affected
   - Recurring regressions (issues that were fixed and came back)
   - Common dependency issues

4. **Cross-reference with codebase** — Check affected code paths for:
   - Missing error handling
   - Race conditions
   - Resource leaks
   - Configuration issues

Report impact assessment for each issue with severity classification."
    - agent: reviewer
      prompt: "# Phase 4: Findings Report

Consolidate all Sentry findings into a structured report.

**Issue List & Categories:**
$INPUT_1

**Root Cause Analysis:**
$INPUT_2

**Impact Analysis:**
$INPUT_3

## Format

```markdown
## Sentry Issues Report

**Project:** [name]
**Date:** [current date]
**Total Issues:** [count]

### Summary

| Severity | Count |
|----------|-------|
| 🔴 Fatal/Critical | X |
| 🟠 Error          | X |
| 🟡 Warning        | X |
| 🔵 Info           | X |

### Top Issues

#### [SENTRY-001] [Issue Title / Short ID]
- **Level:** fatal / error / warning
- **Events:** [count] | **Users affected:** [count]
- **First seen:** [date] | **Last seen:** [date]
- **File:** `path/to/file.ts:42`
- **Root Cause:** [Seer explanation summary]
- **Impact:** [User-facing and system impact]

[...repeat for each top issue...]

### Patterns & Trends

[Common root causes, affected modules, recurring issues]
```

Produce the complete findings report following this format."
    - agent: planner
      prompt: "# Phase 5: Fix Plan

Create a prioritized, actionable plan to fix the Sentry issues.

**Findings Report:**
$INPUT

## Steps

1. **Group related fixes** — Issues with the same root cause should be fixed together.

2. **For complex issues**, reference `sentry issue plan <short-id>` output where relevant.

3. **Include verification steps** — How to confirm each fix resolves the issue in Sentry.

## Format

```markdown
## Sentry Fix Plan

### Priority 1: Critical Fixes (Do Immediately)

#### [Fix for SENTRY-001]
- **Issue(s):** [short-id(s)]
- **What:** [Concise description of the fix]
- **Where:** `path/to/file.ts`
- **How:** [Step-by-step implementation]
- **Verification:** [How to confirm the fix — check Sentry for issue resolution, add test, etc.]

### Priority 2: High-Impact Fixes (This Sprint)
[...]

### Priority 3: Medium-Term Improvements
[...]

### Monitoring Recommendations

- [ ] Set up Sentry alerts for [specific conditions]
- [ ] Add custom breadcrumbs for [specific flows]
- [ ] Configure performance monitoring for [specific transactions]
- [ ] Review error budgets and set SLOs
```

Produce the complete fix plan following this format."

performance:
  description: "Performance optimization — profile bottlenecks, stress-test, and build an optimization plan"
  steps:
    - agent: scout
      prompt: "# Phase 0: Performance Discovery

Before optimizing anything, understand what we're working with.

## Steps

1. **Identify the stack** — Read manifest files (`package.json`, `Cargo.toml`, `requirements.txt`, `go.mod`, etc.) to identify:
   - Language(s), runtime, and framework(s)
   - Build tooling and bundlers (Webpack, Vite, esbuild, Turbopack, etc.)
   - Key dependencies and their versions

2. **Map entry points and hot paths** — Identify:
   - Server entry points, route handlers, middleware chains
   - Client entry points, page routes, critical rendering paths
   - Background jobs, workers, scheduled tasks, queue consumers
   - Database access patterns (ORMs, raw queries, connection setup)

3. **Inventory existing perf tooling** — Check for:
   - Monitoring/APM (Sentry, Datadog, New Relic, Lighthouse CI)
   - Caching layers (Redis, Memcached, in-memory caches, CDN config)
   - Build optimizations (code splitting, tree shaking, minification, compression)
   - Load balancing, auto-scaling, or concurrency configuration

4. **Establish baseline metrics** — Note current state of:
   - Bundle sizes (if frontend)
   - Dependency count and tree depth
   - Number of API routes / endpoints
   - Database migration count and schema complexity
   - Any existing benchmarks or perf test suites

$INPUT

Report your findings in a clear, structured format."
    - agent: reviewer
      prompt: "# Phase 1: Deep Performance Scan

Perform a methodical, file-by-file performance audit. For each finding, record: **file path, line number(s), severity (critical/high/medium/low), category, and description.**

**Project Context:**
$INPUT

## 1.1 Database & Query Performance

Scan for:
- **N+1 queries** — database calls inside loops instead of batch/join operations
- **Missing indexes** — queries on unindexed fields (check schema + query patterns)
- **Unbounded queries** — `SELECT *` or queries without `LIMIT` that could return massive result sets
- **Missing pagination** — list endpoints returning all records
- **Inefficient joins** — cartesian products, unnecessary subqueries, missing query optimization
- **Connection pool misconfiguration** — pool too small for load, no idle timeout, missing connection reuse
- **Missing query caching** — repeated identical queries without caching layer
- **Slow migrations** — locking migrations on large tables, missing concurrent index creation

## 1.2 Compute & I/O Bottlenecks

Scan for:
- **Blocking I/O** — synchronous file reads, blocking network calls on main/event-loop thread
- **CPU-bound work on hot paths** — JSON parsing of large payloads, regex on untrusted input, heavy computation without worker threads
- **Unnecessary serialization** — repeated JSON.stringify/parse cycles, deep cloning where shallow would suffice
- **Missing streaming** — loading entire files/responses into memory instead of streaming
- **Inefficient algorithms** — O(n²) or worse where O(n log n) or O(n) alternatives exist
- **Redundant work** — recomputing values that could be memoized or cached
- **Missing concurrency** — sequential awaits that could be parallelized with Promise.all/allSettled

## 1.3 Frontend & Rendering Performance

Scan for:
- **Redundant re-renders** — components re-rendering without memoization, missing useMemo/useCallback/React.memo
- **Large bundles** — importing entire libraries when only a subset is needed, no tree-shaking
- **Missing code splitting** — no lazy loading for routes or heavy components
- **Unoptimized assets** — uncompressed images, unminified JS/CSS, missing WebP/AVIF
- **Layout thrashing** — reading and writing DOM layout properties in tight loops
- **Missing virtual scrolling** — rendering thousands of list items instead of virtualizing
- **Render-blocking resources** — synchronous scripts in head, CSS not deferred for below-fold content
- **Missing preloading** — critical resources not preloaded, no resource hints (prefetch, preconnect)

## 1.4 Network & Caching

Scan for:
- **Missing HTTP caching** — no Cache-Control headers, no ETag/Last-Modified, no CDN caching
- **Waterfall requests** — sequential API calls that could be batched or parallelized
- **Over-fetching** — API responses returning much more data than the client needs
- **Missing compression** — no gzip/brotli on API responses or static assets
- **Chatty protocols** — many small requests where a single batch request would suffice
- **Missing connection reuse** — not using HTTP keep-alive, creating new connections per request
- **No request deduplication** — identical concurrent requests not deduplicated

## 1.5 Memory & Resource Management

Scan for:
- **Memory leaks** — unclosed resources, event listener accumulation, uncleared timers, orphaned subscriptions
- **Cache without eviction** — in-memory caches that grow unbounded with no TTL, LRU, or size limit
- **Buffer accumulation** — streams or buffers that accumulate without draining
- **Large object retention** — holding references to large objects longer than necessary
- **Missing garbage collection hints** — (where applicable) not releasing references to allow GC

Report findings with file paths, line numbers, severity, category, and description."
    - agent: red-team
      prompt: "# Phase 2: Load & Stress Analysis

Analyze the codebase for breaking points under load. For each finding, record: **file path, line number(s), severity (critical/high/medium/low), category, and description.**

**Project Context:**
$INPUT_1

## 2.1 Concurrency & Contention

Scan for:
- **Race conditions** — shared mutable state accessed from concurrent contexts without synchronization
- **Lock contention** — mutexes, semaphores, or database locks held too long or too broadly
- **Deadlock potential** — multiple locks acquired in inconsistent order
- **Thread pool exhaustion** — blocking operations consuming all available threads/workers
- **Missing atomic operations** — read-modify-write sequences that aren't atomic

## 2.2 Resource Exhaustion Under Load

Scan for:
- **Unbounded queues** — in-memory queues or buffers that grow without limit under backpressure
- **Connection pool exhaustion** — pools too small for peak load, no queuing or timeout for pool acquisition
- **File descriptor leaks** — handles not released on error paths, especially under high concurrency
- **Memory pressure** — allocations that scale linearly with request count without bounds
- **Missing backpressure** — producers faster than consumers with no flow control mechanism
- **Goroutine/fiber/task leaks** — spawned concurrent tasks that never complete or get cleaned up

## 2.3 Scalability Bottlenecks

Scan for:
- **Single points of contention** — shared resources that become bottlenecks (single DB connection, global locks, shared counters)
- **Missing horizontal scaling support** — in-memory sessions, local file storage, node-specific state
- **Thundering herd** — cache stampede on expiry, all instances retrying simultaneously on failure
- **Missing rate limiting** — no throttling on expensive operations, allowing resource exhaustion via legitimate traffic
- **Inefficient serialization under load** — serialization formats that degrade with payload size (XML vs protobuf)

## 2.4 Timeout & Failure Cascade

Scan for:
- **Missing timeouts** — HTTP requests, database queries, or external calls with no timeout configured
- **No circuit breakers** — failing external dependencies causing cascading failures
- **Missing retry budgets** — unlimited retries amplifying load during partial outages
- **No graceful degradation** — system fails completely instead of degrading specific features
- **Health check gaps** — no liveness/readiness probes, or probes that don't check actual dependencies

Report findings with file paths, line numbers, severity, category, and description."
    - agent: reviewer
      prompt: "# Phase 3: Performance Findings Report

Consolidate all performance findings into a structured report.

**Deep Scan Findings:**
$INPUT_2

**Load & Stress Findings:**
$INPUT_3

## Format

```markdown
## Performance Findings Report

**Project:** [name]
**Stack:** [detected languages, frameworks, key deps]
**Scanned:** [number of files] files across [number of directories] directories
**Date:** [current date]

### Summary

| Severity | Count |
|----------|-------|
| 🔴 Critical | X |
| 🟠 High     | X |

| 🔵 Low      | X |

### Critical Findings

#### [PERF-001] [Title]
- **File:** `path/to/file.ts:42`

- **Description:** [What's slow or inefficient]
- **Impact:** [Estimated performance impact — latency, throughput, memory, bundle size]
- **Evidence:** [Code snippet, query plan, or metric reference]

[...repeat for each finding, grouped by severity...]
```

Produce the complete findings report following this format."

Based on the performance findings report, create a prioritized, actionable optimization plan.

**Findings Report:**
$INPUT

## Format

Produce the complete optimization plan following this format."

secure:
  description: "AI security sweep — detect prompt injection vulnerabilities, credential exposure, and missing protections"
  steps:
    - agent: scout
      prompt: "# Phase 0: AI Security Discovery

Map this project's AI attack surface.

1. Identify AI service imports (openai, anthropic, langchain, cohere, huggingface, vercel ai sdk, google generative-ai)
2. Find AI API calls (chat.completions.create, messages.create, generateText, etc.)
3. Find AI-related env vars and endpoints (/api/chat, /api/completion, /api/ai)
4. Map data flow: where user input enters, how it reaches AI calls, what happens with responses
5. Check for input validation, output filtering, rate limiting, auth on AI endpoints
6. Check secrets management: are keys in env vars, is .env in .gitignore

$INPUT

Report all findings with file paths and line numbers."
    - agent: red-team
      prompt: "# Phase 1: AI Security Vulnerability Analysis

Based on the discovery, identify specific attack vectors.

**Discovery Results:**
$INPUT

## Evaluate:
1. **Prompt Injection** — can users inject instructions that override system prompts? Is there input/instruction separation? Indirect injection via databases, URLs, files?
2. **Credential Exfiltration** — can injection reveal API keys? Can tool calling send data externally? Can system prompts be extracted?
3. **Data Leakage** — can users access other users' data? Is PII flowing unprotected? Are responses logged insecurely?
4. **Abuse** — rate limits? Billing limits? Token exhaustion protection?
5. **Output Safety** — is AI output sanitized before HTML rendering? Can output be eval'd as code?

For each vulnerability: file, line, severity, proof of concept, and impact."
    - agent: reviewer
      prompt: "# Phase 2: AI Security Findings Report

Consolidate findings into a structured report.

**Discovery:**
$INPUT_1

**Vulnerabilities:**
$INPUT_2

Format as:
- Security Score X/100
- Executive Summary
- Findings table (severity, count)
- Each finding: ID, severity, category (Prompt Injection / Credential Exposure / Data Leakage / Missing Protection), file:line, description, proof of concept, impact, recommendation
- Positive findings (security measures already in place)"
    - agent: planner
      prompt: "# Phase 3: AI Security Hardening Plan

Create prioritized remediation with installable protections.

**Security Report:**
$INPUT

## Plan:
1. **Priority 1: Critical Fixes** — immediate code changes with file:line and code examples
2. **Priority 2: Install Protections** — recommend running /secure install to generate AI Security Guard (input sanitization + output filtering), Security Policy YAML, rate limiting middleware, and CI security checks
3. **Priority 3: Configuration** — rate limits, token limits, logging, monitoring
4. **Priority 4: Process** — security review checklist, incident response, alerting

Recommended architecture: User Input -> Rate Limiter -> Input Sanitizer -> AI API -> Output Filter -> Response, with Audit Logger and Content Filter as side-channels."

code-review:
  description: "Multi-pass code review — parallel context gathering, split review, remediation, validation, test verification, and final report"
  steps:
    - agent: scout
      prompt: "# Step 1: Architecture Scout — Deep Structural Research

Map the high-level architecture of the code under review. Do NOT skim — read files thoroughly.

## Scope

The user's request determines what to review. Parse their intent:
- If they mention 'last commit' or 'HEAD~1', run `git diff HEAD~1` and focus on those files
- If they mention 'staged' or 'cached', run `git diff --cached` and focus on those files
- If they mention 'unstaged' or 'current changes', run `git diff` and focus on those files
- If they mention a specific directory or file path, focus on that
- If they say 'full' or 'everything', scan the entire project
- If unclear, default to `git diff` (unstaged changes)

User request: $ORIGINAL

## Tasks

1. **Identify the change scope** — run the appropriate git diff command and list all affected files
2. **Deep-read every changed file end-to-end** — do not skim. For each changed file, identify:
   - What module/subsystem it belongs to
   - Its entry points and exports
   - Key interfaces, types, enums, and data structures it defines or uses
   - Base classes and inheritance chains it participates in
3. **Identify the tech stack** — languages, frameworks, build tools, runtime
4. **Map the module boundaries** — how changed files relate to each other and to the rest of the codebase
5. **Map the class/type hierarchy** — for any new classes, interfaces, enums, or types introduced in the diff:
   - Search the ENTIRE codebase for existing base classes, abstract classes, or interfaces that could have been extended instead
   - Search for existing enums that could have been extended with new values instead of creating new enums
   - Search for existing utility functions/helpers that already do what the new code does
   - Run targeted grep/find for similar names, similar functionality, similar patterns
6. **Catalog reusable infrastructure** — identify shared utilities, common base classes, helper libraries, and framework abstractions that already exist in the project

Report findings with file paths and line numbers. Be thorough — the downstream review depends on the depth of your research."
    - agent: ranger
      prompt: "# Step 2: Pattern, Convention & DRY Scout — Deep Research

Deeply analyze coding patterns and enforce DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principles. This is the most critical scout — your findings determine whether the code is extending the codebase properly or reinventing the wheel.

User request: $ORIGINAL

## Tasks

1. **Identify the change scope** — run the appropriate git diff command based on the user's request (see Step 1 for scope parsing rules) and list all affected files

2. **Study existing examples FIRST** — before judging the changed code, deeply research the codebase:
   - Find 3-5 existing files that do similar things to the changed files
   - Read those examples end-to-end to understand the established patterns
   - Note the naming conventions, file organization, error handling, and code structure used
   - Identify the 'golden example' — the best-written existing file that the new code should emulate

3. **DRY Enforcement — the core mission:**
   - **New files created?** Search the codebase for existing files that already solve the same problem or a similar one. Could the existing file have been extended instead?
   - **New classes/interfaces?** Search for existing base classes, abstract classes, mixins, or interfaces that the new class should extend or implement. Run `grep -r 'class ' --include='*.ts'` (or equivalent) to find all classes.
   - **New enums or constants?** Search for existing enums that could have received new values instead of creating a new enum. Run `grep -r 'enum ' --include='*.ts'` to find all enums.
   - **New utility functions?** Search for existing helpers, utils, and shared libraries. Run `find . -name '*util*' -o -name '*helper*' -o -name '*common*' -o -name '*shared*'` to locate them. Read them.
   - **New types?** Search for existing type definitions that could be extended, intersected, or reused.
   - **Duplicated logic?** For any block of 5+ lines in the changed code, search the codebase for similar logic that already exists elsewhere.
   - For every DRY violation found, report: the new code location, the existing code it duplicates or should extend, and the specific refactoring suggested.

4. **Catalog existing patterns** in the changed files and their surrounding context:
   - Naming conventions (variables, functions, files, classes)
   - Error handling patterns (try/catch, Result types, error callbacks)
   - Async patterns (async/await, promises, callbacks, observables)
   - State management patterns
   - Import/export organization
   - How similar features were added before (find the git log for analogous past changes if possible)

5. **Identify pattern violations** — places where the changed code breaks established conventions

6. **Check code style** — formatting, indentation, comment style, documentation patterns

7. **Look for anti-patterns** — copy-paste duplication, god objects, deep nesting, magic numbers, dead code

## Output: DRY Violations Section (REQUIRED)

You MUST include a dedicated DRY Violations section in your report:

```
### DRY Violations

| New Code | Existing Code | Action |
|----------|--------------|--------|
| path/new.ts:15 NewClass | path/existing.ts:30 BaseClass | Extend BaseClass instead of creating NewClass |
| path/new.ts:45 StatusEnum | path/types.ts:10 StateEnum | Add values to StateEnum instead |
| path/new.ts:80 formatDate() | path/utils.ts:20 formatTimestamp() | Reuse formatTimestamp() |
```

If no DRY violations found, explicitly state: 'No DRY violations detected — all new code is justified.'

Report all findings with file paths and line numbers."
    - agent: scout
      prompt: "# Step 3: Dependency, Configuration & Secrets Scout — Deep Research

Analyze dependencies, configuration, and secrets exposure in the code under review. Research thoroughly before reporting.

User request: $ORIGINAL

## Tasks

### Dependency Analysis
1. **Identify the change scope** — run the appropriate git diff command based on the user's request and list all affected files
2. **Map dependency changes** — check if any manifest files changed (package.json, Cargo.toml, requirements.txt, go.mod, etc.):
   - New dependencies added — are they necessary? Are they well-maintained? Does the project already have a dependency that does the same thing?
   - Dependencies removed — is anything still importing them?
   - Version changes — any breaking changes or known CVEs?
3. **DRY at the dependency level** — for any new external dependency added:
   - Search the existing codebase for similar functionality already provided by current dependencies
   - Check if the project already has a wrapper/abstraction for this kind of functionality
   - Determine if a small utility function would suffice instead of adding a full dependency
4. **Check configuration changes** — env files, config files, build configs, CI/CD pipelines
5. **Trace import chains** — for each changed file, map what it imports and what imports it
   - Are there existing shared modules that should be imported instead of duplicating logic?
   - Are imports organized consistently with the rest of the codebase?
6. **Identify circular dependencies** — check for import cycles involving changed files

### Secrets Scanning (CRITICAL)
7. **Scan ALL changed files line-by-line** for secrets exposure:
   - **API keys** — patterns like `AKIA`, `sk-`, `sk_live_`, `pk_live_`, `ghp_`, `gho_`, `github_pat_`, `xoxb-`, `xoxp-`
   - **Tokens & passwords** — any string assigned to variables named `token`, `secret`, `password`, `passwd`, `api_key`, `apiKey`, `auth`, `credential`
   - **Connection strings** — database URIs (`mongodb://`, `postgres://`, `mysql://`, `redis://`), DSNs (`https://*.ingest.sentry.io`)
   - **Private keys** — `BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY`, `BEGIN OPENSSH PRIVATE KEY`, `BEGIN EC PRIVATE KEY`, `BEGIN PGP PRIVATE KEY`
   - **Cloud credentials** — AWS access keys, GCP service account JSON, Azure connection strings
   - **JWT secrets** — hardcoded JWT signing keys or HMAC secrets
   - **Webhook URLs** — Slack webhooks (`hooks.slack.com`), Discord webhooks, generic callback URLs with tokens in query params
   - **High-entropy strings** — any suspicious base64 or hex string longer than 32 characters assigned to a constant
8. **Scan git history of changed files** — run `git log --diff-filter=D -p -- <file>` on changed files to check if secrets were added then removed (still in history)
9. **Check .env and config files** — verify:
   - `.env` files are in `.gitignore`
   - `.env.example` or `.env.template` exists with placeholder values (not real secrets)
   - No `.env` files are tracked in git (`git ls-files '*.env'`)
   - Config files don't contain inline secrets
10. **Check for secrets in logs** — scan changed code for logging statements that might output sensitive data:
    - `console.log`, `logger.info/debug/error`, `print`, `fmt.Println` statements that include tokens, passwords, headers, or request bodies
    - Error messages that might leak internal paths, stack traces, or credentials
11. **Verify secrets management** — check if the project uses proper secrets management:
    - Environment variables for runtime secrets
    - Secret managers (AWS Secrets Manager, Vault, 1Password CLI)
    - Encrypted config files
    - `.gitignore` entries for sensitive files

## Output: Secrets Report Section (REQUIRED)

```
### Secrets Scan Results

**Status:** CLEAN / FINDINGS DETECTED

| Severity | File | Line | Type | Detail |
|----------|------|------|------|--------|
| CRITICAL | path/file.ts | 15 | API Key | Hardcoded Stripe key `sk_live_...` |
| HIGH | path/config.ts | 30 | Connection String | Postgres URI with embedded password |
| MEDIUM | path/logger.ts | 45 | Log Leak | Auth header logged in debug mode |

### .env / Gitignore Status
- .env in .gitignore: YES/NO
- .env.example exists: YES/NO
- Tracked .env files: [list or NONE]

### Secrets Management Assessment
[How the project handles secrets — good practices and gaps]
```

Report ALL findings with file paths and line numbers. Secrets exposure is a blocking issue — never downplay it."
    - agent: scout

audit:
  description: "Comprehensive code audit — scans project, finds issues, generates report and hardening plan"
  steps:
    - agent: scout
      prompt: "# Phase 0: Project Discovery

Before touching any code, understand what we're working with.

## Steps

1. **Scan the project root** — Read `package.json`, `Cargo.toml`, `requirements.txt`, `go.mod`, `build.gradle`, `Podfile`, or any manifest files to identify:
   - Language(s) and runtime (Node.js, Python, Rust, Go, Swift, Kotlin, etc.)
   - Framework(s) (React, Next.js, Express, FastAPI, Capacitor, Flutter, etc.)
   - Key dependencies and their versions
   - Build tooling and bundlers

2. **Map the architecture** — Identify:
   - Entry points (servers, main files, route handlers, app delegates)
   - Data layer (databases, ORMs, caches, queues)
   - External integrations (APIs, SDKs, third-party services)
   - Auth and session management patterns
   - Background jobs, workers, or scheduled tasks

3. **Classify the project type** and load the appropriate best-practice lens:
   - **Web API / Backend Service** → OWASP API Top 10, 12-factor app principles
   - **Frontend SPA / SSR** → XSS prevention, CSP, bundle security, hydration safety
   - **Mobile App (iOS/Android/Capacitor)** → Platform security guidelines, secure storage, deep link validation
   - **CLI Tool / Agent System** → Input sanitization, privilege escalation, subprocess safety
   - **Library / SDK** → Supply chain safety, API surface minimization, semver discipline
   - **Monorepo / Multi-service** → Audit each service independently, then cross-service boundaries

4. **Search for current best practices** — Based on the identified stack, search the web for:
   - `"{framework} security best practices {current_year}"`
   - `"{language} memory leak patterns"`
   - `"{framework} performance optimization"`
   - Known CVEs for detected dependency versions
   - Official security hardening guides for the framework

$INPUT

Report your findings in a clear, structured format."
    - agent: reviewer
      prompt: "# Phase 1: Deep Scan — Memory, Patterns, Performance, Resilience

Perform a methodical, file-by-file audit. For each finding, record: **file path, line number(s), severity (critical/high/medium/low), category, and description.**

**Project Context:**
$INPUT

## 1.1 Memory Leaks & Resource Management

Scan for:
- **Unclosed resources** — database connections, file handles, streams, sockets, WebSocket connections not properly closed or disposed
- **Event listener accumulation** — listeners added in loops, on mount, or in constructors without corresponding removal on teardown/unmount/dispose
- **Uncleared timers** — `setInterval`, `setTimeout`, cron jobs, or polling loops without cleanup
- **Orphaned subscriptions** — RxJS/Observable/EventEmitter subscriptions without unsubscribe logic
- **Circular references** — objects referencing each other preventing garbage collection
- **Cache without eviction** — in-memory caches (`Map`, `Set`, objects) that grow unbounded with no TTL, LRU, or size limit
- **Closure captures** — closures inadvertently capturing large scopes or DOM references
- **Buffer accumulation** — streams or buffers that accumulate without draining
- **Global state pollution** — data appended to global/module-level variables across requests or invocations
- **Detached DOM nodes** — references to DOM elements that have been removed from the tree (frontend)
- **Native bridge leaks** — (mobile) native plugin callbacks not cleaned up, Capacitor/Cordova listener leaks

## 1.2 Anti-Patterns & Code Smells

Scan for:
- **Error swallowing** — empty catch blocks, `.catch(() => {})`, ignored promise rejections
- **Unhandled async** — missing `await`, fire-and-forget promises without error handling, unhandled rejection paths
- **Race conditions** — shared mutable state accessed from concurrent contexts without synchronization
- **Callback hell / pyramid of doom** — deeply nested callbacks that should be refactored to async/await or pipelines
- **God objects / functions** — single files or functions with 500+ lines doing too many things
- **Magic numbers and strings** — hardcoded values without named constants or configuration
- **Copy-paste duplication** — repeated code blocks that should be abstracted
- **Tight coupling** — direct instantiation or deep import chains making testing/mocking impossible
- **Missing type safety** — `any` types in TypeScript, no input validation, implicit type coercion
- **Improper null handling** — unchecked nullable values, missing optional chaining, bare property access on potentially undefined objects
- **Synchronous blocking** — blocking the event loop (Node.js), main thread (mobile), or UI thread with heavy computation
- **Dead code** — unreachable code, unused imports, commented-out blocks, deprecated feature flags still in codebase

## 1.4 Performance & Reliability

Scan for:
- **N+1 queries** — database calls inside loops instead of batch/join operations
- **Missing indexes** — queries on unindexed fields (check schema + query patterns)
- **Unbounded queries** — `SELECT *` or queries without `LIMIT` that could return massive result sets
- **Missing pagination** — list endpoints that return all records
- **Redundant re-renders** — (frontend) components re-rendering without memoization, missing `useMemo`/`useCallback`/`React.memo`
- **Large bundle / payload** — importing entire libraries when only a subset is needed, no tree-shaking, oversized API responses
- **Missing caching** — repeated expensive computations or network calls without caching layers
- **No graceful degradation** — missing circuit breakers, retries, fallbacks, or timeout configurations
- **Missing health checks** — no liveness/readiness endpoints for services
- **Unoptimized assets** — uncompressed images, unminified JS/CSS in production builds

## 1.5 Resilience & Error Handling

Scan for:
- **Missing error boundaries** — (React) no `ErrorBoundary` components around critical UI sections
- **Crash-inducing exceptions** — unhandled exceptions that crash the process/app instead of being caught
- **No retry logic** — network calls to external services without retry + backoff
- **Missing timeouts** — HTTP requests, database queries, or external calls with no timeout configured
- **Incomplete cleanup on failure** — transactions not rolled back, temp files not deleted, locks not released on error paths
- **Silent failures** — operations that fail but the system continues in a corrupt or inconsistent state
- **Missing validation at boundaries** — no input validation on API endpoints, form submissions, or message handlers

Report findings with file paths, line numbers, severity, category, and description."
    - agent: red-team
      prompt: "# Phase 1: Deep Scan — Security Vulnerabilities

Perform a methodical security audit. For each finding, record: **file path, line number(s), severity (critical/high/medium/low), category, and description.**

**Project Context:**
$INPUT_1

## 1.3 Security Vulnerabilities

Scan for:
- **Injection flaws** — SQL injection, NoSQL injection, command injection, LDAP injection, template injection
- **XSS vectors** — unsanitized user input rendered in HTML/DOM, `dangerouslySetInnerHTML`, `innerHTML`, `eval()`
- **Authentication weaknesses** — hardcoded credentials, weak token generation, missing token expiration, insecure session management
- **Authorization gaps** — missing permission checks, IDOR (Insecure Direct Object References), privilege escalation paths
- **Secrets exposure** — API keys, tokens, passwords in source code, `.env` files committed, secrets in logs
- **Insecure data storage** — sensitive data in `localStorage`, `SharedPreferences` without encryption, plaintext storage
- **Insecure communication** — HTTP instead of HTTPS, missing TLS certificate validation, insecure WebSocket connections
- **Dependency vulnerabilities** — outdated packages with known CVEs, unpinned versions, untrusted registries
- **Path traversal** — user-controlled file paths without sanitization
- **CORS misconfiguration** — overly permissive origins, credentials exposure
- **CSRF / SSRF** — missing anti-forgery tokens, unvalidated redirect URLs, internal network access via user-supplied URLs
- **Logging sensitive data** — PII, tokens, or credentials written to logs
- **Insecure deserialization** — parsing untrusted data (JSON, YAML, pickle, XML) without validation
- **Rate limiting absence** — no throttling on auth endpoints, API routes, or resource-intensive operations

Report findings with file paths, line numbers, severity, category, and description."
    - agent: reviewer
      prompt: "# Phase 2: Findings Report

Consolidate the audit findings into a structured report.

**Phase 1 Findings:**

## Code Quality & Performance Findings

$INPUT_2

## Security Findings

$INPUT_3

## Format

```markdown
## Audit Findings Report

**Project:** [name]
**Stack:** [detected languages, frameworks, key deps]
**Scanned:** [number of files] files across [number of directories] directories
**Date:** [current date]

### Summary

| Severity | Count |
|----------|-------|
| 🔴 Critical | X |
| 🟠 High     | X |
| 🟡 Medium   | X |
| 🔵 Low      | X |

### Critical Findings

#### [CRIT-001] [Title]
- **File:** `path/to/file.ts:42`
- **Category:** Memory Leak | Security | Anti-Pattern | Performance | Resilience
- **Description:** [What's wrong]
- **Impact:** [What can go wrong if unaddressed]
- **Evidence:** [Code snippet or reference]

[...repeat for each finding, grouped by severity...]
```

Produce the complete findings report following this format."
    - agent: planner
      prompt: "# Phase 3: Hardening Plan

Based on the findings report, create a prioritized, actionable remediation plan.

**Findings Report:**
$INPUT

## Format

```markdown
## Hardening Plan

### Priority 1: Critical Fixes (Do Immediately)

#### [Fix for CRIT-001]
- **What:** [Concise description of the change]
- **Where:** `path/to/file.ts`
- **How:** [Step-by-step implementation approach]
- **Verification:** [How to confirm the fix works — test, metric, or check]

### Priority 2: High-Impact Improvements (This Sprint)
[...]

### Priority 3: Medium-Term Hardening (Next 2-4 Weeks)
[...]

### Priority 4: Long-Term Excellence (Backlog)
[...]

### Recommended Tooling & Automation

| Purpose | Tool | Notes |
|---------|------|------|
| Static analysis | [e.g., ESLint strict config, Semgrep] | [setup notes] |
| Dependency audit | [e.g., npm audit, Snyk, Dependabot] | [frequency] |
| Memory profiling | [e.g., Chrome DevTools, Instruments, Valgrind] | [when to run] |
| Security scanning | [e.g., OWASP ZAP, Trivy, CodeQL] | [CI integration] |
| Performance monitoring | [e.g., Sentry, Datadog, Lighthouse CI] | [thresholds] |

### Recommended Process Changes

- [ ] Add pre-commit hooks for [specific checks]
- [ ] Add CI pipeline stages for [security scan, lint, type check]
- [ ] Establish code review checklist covering [top finding categories]
- [ ] Schedule recurring dependency audits every [timeframe]
- [ ] Add monitoring/alerting for [specific metrics from findings]
```

Produce the complete hardening plan following this format."
